prevalence.trend: stable; improved recognition and multidisciplinary follow-up

burden.dalyPerCapita: very low (rare), but significant per-patient burden (orthopedic, neurologic, ENT complications)
burden.mortalityRate: Low; increased risk in infancy with severe airway/foramen magnum issues

riskFactors: advanced paternal age (increased de novo risk)
family history (autosomal dominant inheritance when inherited)
vulnerablePopulations: patients with limited access to orthopedic/ENT monitoring
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical-radiographic skeletal dysplasia pattern; confirmatory FGFR3 genetic testing; monitor for foramen magnum stenosis, hydrocephalus, sleep apnea and spinal stenosis

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; diagnosis at birth/early childhood by clinical and imaging features
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Good when multidisciplinary care is available; variable across regions

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Specialist confirmation via genetics/orthopedics; imaging access varies in smaller municipalities
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Multidisciplinary follow-up (orthopedics, neurosurgery, ENT, sleep medicine), management of spinal stenosis and limb alignment, hearing and sleep apnea evaluation; genetic counseling
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Unequal access to specialized centers and timely neurosurgical assessment for foramen magnum stenosis
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Reference hospitals in major capitals can provide multidisciplinary pathways
